The task of securing recognized frontiers was complicated by the fact that, between 1777 and 1801, Portugal and Spain had annulled their previous treaties setting out the borders between their American colonial empires.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002301-182) However, the Empire was able to sign several bilateral treaties with neighbors, including Uruguay (1851), Peru (1851 and 1874), the [Republic of New Granada](/wiki/Republic_of_New_Granada "Republic of New Granada") (later Colombia, 1853), Venezuela (1859), Bolivia (1867) and Paraguay (1872).[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994525-183)[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002302-184) By 1889, most of its borders were firmly established. The remaining issues—including the purchase of the region of [Acre](/wiki/Acre_(state) "Acre (state)") from Bolivia which would give Brazil its present-day configuration[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994578-185)—were only finally resolved after the country became a republic.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994575-186)

A number of conflicts occurred between the Empire and its neighbors. Brazil experienced no serious conflicts with its neighbors to the north and west, due to the buffer of the nearly impenetrable and sparsely populated Amazonian rainforest.[[B]](#cite_note-187) In the south, however, the colonial disputes inherited from Portugal and Spain over the control of the navigable rivers and plains which formed the frontiers continued after independence.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002329-188) The lack of mutually agreed borders in this area led to several international conflicts, from the [Cisplatine War](/wiki/Cisplatine_War "Cisplatine War") to the [Paraguayan War](/wiki/Paraguayan_War "Paraguayan War").[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002323–324-189)

"Brazil is, next to ourselves, the great power on the American continent", affirmed [James Watson Webb](/wiki/James_Watson_Webb "James Watson Webb"), the US minister to Brazil, in 1867.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith20107-190) The Empire's rise was noticed as early as 1844 by [John C. Calhoun](/wiki/John_C._Calhoun "John C. Calhoun"), the US Secretary of State: "Next to the United States, Brazil is the most wealthy, the greatest and the most firmly established of all the American powers."[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith201018-191) By the early 1870s,[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_29-83) the international reputation of the Empire of Brazil had improved considerably, and it remained well-regarded internationally until its end in 1889.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETopik200056-105) [Christopher Columbus Andrews](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus_Andrews "Christopher Columbus Andrews"), an American diplomat in the Brazilian capital in the 1880s, later recalled Brazil as an "important Empire" in his memoirs.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999306-192) In 1871, Brazil was invited to arbitrate the dispute between the United States and Britain which became known as the [*Alabama* Claims](/wiki/Alabama_Claims "Alabama Claims"). In 1880, the Empire acted as arbiter between the United States and France over the damage caused to US nationals during the [French intervention in Mexico](/wiki/Second_French_intervention_in_Mexico "Second French intervention in Mexico"). In 1884, Brazil was called upon to arbitrate between Chile and several other nations (namely France, Italy, Britain, Germany, Belgium, Austria-Hungary and Switzerland) over damages arising from the [War of the Pacific](/wiki/War_of_the_Pacific "War of the Pacific").[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodrigues1995208-193)

The Brazilian government eventually felt confident enough to negotiate a trade deal with the United States in 1889, the first to be undertaken with any nation since the disastrous and exploitative trade treaty with Britain in 1826 (canceled in 1844). American historian Steven C. Topik said that Pedro II's "quest for a trade treaty with the United States was part of a grander strategy to increase national sovereignty and autonomy." Unlike the circumstances of the previous pact, the Empire was in a strong position to insist on favorable trade terms, as negotiations occurred during a time of Brazilian domestic prosperity and international prestige.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETopik200060-194)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of the Empire of Brazil](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Empire_of_Brazil "Economy of the Empire of Brazil")

### Currency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Currency")]

Main article: [Brazilian real (old)](/wiki/Brazilian_real_(old) "Brazilian real (old)")

[![Photograph of a banknote containing a picture of a bearded man in the center and the number 500 printed in the corners](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Papel-moeda_-_500_r%C3%A9is.jpg/250px-Papel-moeda_-_500_r%C3%A9is.jpg)](/wiki/File:Papel-moeda_-_500_r%C3%A9is.jpg)

500 *réis* (royals) or Rs 500

[![Photograph of a banknote containing a picture of a bearded man on the right side and a large printed number 1 on the left side](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Papel-moeda_-_1000_r%C3%A9is.jpg/250px-Papel-moeda_-_1000_r%C3%A9is.jpg)](/wiki/File:Papel-moeda_-_1000_r%C3%A9is.jpg)

1,000 *réis* (royals) or Rs 1$000 or *milréis* (thousand royals)

The unit of currency from the Empire's founding, and until 1942, was the *real* ("royal" in English, its plural form was *réis* and is *reais* in modern Portuguese), and was derived from the [Portuguese real](/wiki/Portuguese_real "Portuguese real"). It was usually called *milréis* (English: thousand royals) and written as 1$000. A thousand *milréis* (1:000$000)—or one million *réis*—was known as *conto de réis*.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999XVI-195) One *conto de réis* was represented by the symbol *Rs* written before the value and by a dollar sign was written before any amounts lower than 1,000 *réis*. Thus, 350 *réis* was written as "Rs 350"; 1,712 *réis* as "Rs 1$712"; and 1,020,800 *réis* was written as "Rs 1:020$800". For millions, a period was used as a separator between millions, billions, trillions, etc. (e.g., 1 billion *réis* was written as "Rs 1.000:000$000"). A colon functioned to separate millions from thousands, and the $ sign (typically written as [![Dollar sign with two vertical lines](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Cifr%C3%A3o_symbol_%28narrow%29.svg/20px-Cifr%C3%A3o_symbol_%28narrow%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cifr%C3%A3o_symbol_(narrow).svg)) was inserted between thousands and hundreds (999 or fewer).[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraça_Filho200421-196)

### Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Overview")]

[![A photograph showing the interior of a large factory building with lighting provided by overhead skylights, underneath which is suspended a line shaft providing power via pulley belts to various machines on the factory floor, some of which dwarf workers standing by their stations](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Fabrica_brasil_1880.jpg/250px-Fabrica_brasil_1880.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fabrica_brasil_1880.jpg)

A Brazilian factory, 1880

Brazil's [international trade](/wiki/International_trade "International trade") reached a total value of Rs 79.000:000$000 between 1834 and 1839. This continued to increase every year until it reached Rs 472.000:000$000 between 1886 and 1887: an annual growth rate of 3.88% since 1839.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESodré2004201-197) The absolute value of exports from the Empire in 1850 was the highest in Latin America, and triple that of Argentina which was in fourth place. Brazil would keep its high standing in exports and general economic growth until the end of the monarchy.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFaustoDevoto200547-198) Brazilian economic expansion, especially after 1850, compared well with that of the United States and European nations.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFaustoDevoto200550-199) The national tax revenue amounted to Rs 11.795:000$000 in 1831 and rose to Rs 160.840:000$000 in 1889. By 1858, national tax revenues ranked as the eighth-largest in the world.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_1200-200) Imperial Brazil was, despite its progress, a country where wealth was very unequally distributed.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988218,_236,_237-201) However, for purposes of comparison, according to historian Steven C. Topik, in the United States, "by 1890, 80 percent of the population lived on the margin of subsistence, while 20 percent controlled almost all wealth."[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETopik200019-202)

[![A photograph showing workers spreading or gathering coffee beans drying on a large paved plaza with an elegant, two-story neoclassical building on the left and warehouses and other plantation buildings in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Fazenda_Santa_Genebra_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_1880.jpg/250px-Fazenda_Santa_Genebra_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_1880.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fazenda_Santa_Genebra_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_1880.jpg)

A coffee farm in São Paulo province, 1880

As new technologies appeared, and with increases in internal productivity, exports increased considerably. This made it possible to reach equilibrium in the [balance of trade](/wiki/Balance_of_trade "Balance of trade"). During the 1820s sugar constituted about 30% of total exports while cotton constituted 21%, coffee 18% and leather and [skins](/wiki/Skinning "Skinning") 14%. Twenty years later, coffee would reach 42%, sugar 27%, leather and skins 9%, and cotton 8% of the total exports. This did not mean a reduction in the production of any of these items and, in fact, the opposite occurred. Growth occurred in all sectors, some more than others. In the period between 1820 and 1840, Fausto says "Brazilian exports had doubled in volume and had tripled in nominal value" while the valuation denominated in [pounds sterling](/wiki/Pounds_sterling "Pounds sterling") increased by over 40%.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFaustoDevoto200546-203) Brazil was not the only country where agriculture played an important role on exports. Around 1890, in the United States, by then the richest nation in the Americas, agricultural goods represented 80% of all its exports.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETopik200033-204)

In the 1820s, Brazil exported 11,000 tons of [cacao](/wiki/Theobroma_cacao "Theobroma cacao") and by 1880 this had increased to 73,500 tons.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002250-205) Between 1821 and 1825, 41,174 tons of sugar were exported, rising to 238,074 tons between 1881 and 1885.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002251-206) Until 1850, rubber production was insignificant, but between 1881 and 1890, it had reached third place among Brazilian exports.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFausto1995239-207) This was about 81 tons between 1827 and 1830 reaching 1,632 tons in 1852. By 1900 the country was exporting 24,301,452 tons of rubber.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002250-205) Brazil also exported around 3,377,000 tons of coffee between 1821 and 1860 while between 1861 and 1889 this reached 6,804,000 tons.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon2002368-208) Technological innovations also contributed to the growth of exports,[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFaustoDevoto200546-203) in particular the adoption of [steam navigation](/wiki/Steam_navigation "Steam navigation") and railroads allowed for faster and more convenient cargo transportation.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002538-209)

[![Photograph of a steam locomotive pulling passenger cars and sitting at the platform outside a large station building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Railroad_station_brazil_1885.jpg/250px-Railroad_station_brazil_1885.jpg)](/wiki/File:Railroad_station_brazil_1885.jpg)

Railroad station in São Paulo province (Brazilian southeast), c. 1885

[![Photograph of a steam locomotive and train sitting on a curved section of track next to a curved platform and station building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Railroad_station_in_minas_gerais_1884.jpg/250px-Railroad_station_in_minas_gerais_1884.jpg)](/wiki/File:Railroad_station_in_minas_gerais_1884.jpg)

A railroad station in Minas Gerais province (Brazilian southeast), c. 1884

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Development")]

Further information: [Rail transport in Brazil](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Brazil "Rail transport in Brazil")

Development on an immense scale occurred during this period, anticipating similar advancements in European countries.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_213-210)[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVasquez200738-211) In 1850, there were fifty factories with a total [capital](/wiki/Capital_(economics) "Capital (economics)") of Rs 7.000:000$000. At the end of the Imperial period in 1889, Brazil had 636 factories representing an annual rate of increase of 6.74% over the number in 1850, and with a total capital of approximately Rs 401.630:600$000 (which represents an annual growth rate in value of 10.94% from 1850 to 1889).[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994496-212) The "countryside echoed with the clang of iron track being laid as railroads were constructed at the most furious pace of the 19th century; indeed, building in 1880s was the second greatest in absolute terms in Brazil's entire history. Only eight countries in the entire world laid more track in the decade than Brazil."[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETopik200056-105) The first railroad line, with only 15 kilometres (9.3 mi) of track, was opened on 30 April 1854[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon2002222-213) at a time when some European countries still had no rail service.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_213-210) By 1868, there were 718 kilometres (446 mi) of railroad lines,[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon2002225-214) and by the end of the empire in 1889 this had grown to 9,200 kilometres (5,700 mi) with another 9,000 kilometres (5,600 mi) under construction[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon2002226-215) making it the country with "the largest rail network in Latin America".[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETopik200056-105)

Factories were constructed throughout the Empire in the 1880s, allowing Brazil's cities to be modernized and "receive the benefits of gas, electrical, sanitation, telegraph, and tram companies. Brazil was entering the modern world."[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETopik200056-105) It was the fifth country in the world to install modern city [sewers](/wiki/Sanitary_sewer "Sanitary sewer"), the third to have [sewage treatment](/wiki/Sewage_treatment "Sewage treatment")[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_213-210) and one of the pioneers in the installation of a telephone service.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_2309-216) In addition to the foregoing improvements to infrastructure, it was also the first South American nation to adopt public electric lighting (in 1883)[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002539-217) and the second in the Americas (behind the United States) to establish a transatlantic [telegraphic](/wiki/Telegraph "Telegraph") line connecting it directly to Europe in 1874.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_213-210) The first domestic telegraph line appeared during 1852 in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"). By 1889, there were 18,925 kilometres (11,759 mi) of telegraph lines connecting the country's capital to distant Brazilian provinces such as [Pará](/wiki/Par%C3%A1 "Pará") and even linking to other South American countries such as [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") and [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay").[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon2002366-218)

## Society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Society")]

### Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [1872 Brazilian census](/wiki/1872_Brazilian_census "1872 Brazilian census")

Since the second half of the 18th century, when Brazil was still a colony, the government had attempted to gather data regarding the population. However, few captaincies (later called provinces) collected the requested information.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002131-219) After independence the government instituted a commission for statistics in an 1829 decree with a mandate to hold a national census.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002131-219) The commission was a failure and was disbanded in 1834. In the ensuing years, provincial governments were tasked with collecting census information, but their census reports were often incomplete or not submitted at all.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002131-219) In 1851, another attempt at a nationwide census failed when rioting broke out. This was the result of the erroneous belief among Brazilians of mixed-race descent that the survey was a subterfuge designed to enslave anyone having African blood.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002132-220)

The first true national census with exhaustive and broad coverage was carried out [in 1872](/wiki/1872_Brazilian_Census "1872 Brazilian Census"). The small number of people and small number of towns reported by the census reveal Brazil's enormous territory to have been sparsely populated. It showed Brazil as having a total population of 9,930,478 inhabitants.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002132-220) Estimates made by the government in prior decades showed 4,000,000 inhabitants in 1823 and gave a figure of 7,000,700 in 1854.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002132-220) The population was distributed across 20 [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Brazil "Provinces of Brazil") and the [Neutral Municipality](/wiki/Neutral_Municipality "Neutral Municipality") (the Empire's capital) with 641 municipalities.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002132-220)

Among the free population 23.4% of males and 13.4% of females were considered literate.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002133-243) Men represented 52% (5,123,869) of the total population.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002133-243) Figures for the population by age showed 24.6% were children younger than 10 years old; 21.1% were between 11 and 20; 32.9% were between 21 and 40; 8.4% were between 41 and 50; 12.8% were between 51 and 70; and lastly, only 3.4% were over 71.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002133-243) The residents in the combined northeast and southeast regions comprised 87.2% of the nation's population.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaer2002341-244) The second national census was held in 1890 when the Brazilian republic was only a few months old. Its results showed that the population had grown to 14,333,915 inhabitants since the 1872 census.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamos200382-245)

### Ethnic groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Ethnic groups")]

Main article: [Race and ethnicity in Brazil](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_Brazil "Race and ethnicity in Brazil")

See also: [Multiracialism § Brazil](/wiki/Multiracialism#Brazil "Multiracialism")

[![A map of Brazil with regions highlighted using various colors](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Empire_of_Brazil_ethnic_groups_%28edit%29.png/250px-Empire_of_Brazil_ethnic_groups_%28edit%29.png)](/wiki/File:Empire_of_Brazil_ethnic_groups_(edit).png)

This map shows where ethnic groups predominated within Brazil: purple—*caboclos*; brown—*whites*; green—*mulattoes*; white—sparsely populated. **Note**: over 80% of the population lived along the coastline[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaer2002341-244)

Four ethnic groups were recognized in Imperial Brazil: [white](/wiki/White_Brazilian "White Brazilian"), [black](/wiki/Afro-Brazilian "Afro-Brazilian"), [Indian](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Brazil "Indigenous peoples in Brazil"), and [brown](/wiki/Pardo "Pardo").[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamos200382-245) [Brown](/wiki/Brown_people#Pardos_in_Brazil "Brown people") (Portuguese: *pardo*) was a designation for [multiracial Brazilians](/wiki/Multiracial#Brazil "Multiracial") which is still officially used,[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoelho1996268-246)[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVesentini1988117-247) though some scholars prefer the term "mixed one" (Portuguese: *mestiço*). The term denotes a broad category which includes *[caboclos](/wiki/Caboclo "Caboclo")* (descendants of whites and Indians), [mulattoes](/wiki/Mulatto "Mulatto") (descendants of whites and blacks) and *[cafuzos](/wiki/Zambo "Zambo")* (descendants of blacks and Indians).[[246]](#cite_note-248)

[![A montage of old photographic portraits of eighteen individual people arranged in three rows](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Brasileiros_do_seculo_XIX.png/500px-Brasileiros_do_seculo_XIX.png)](/wiki/File:Brasileiros_do_seculo_XIX.png)

19th-century Brazilians. 1st row: [White Brazilians](/wiki/White_Brazilians "White Brazilians"). 2nd row: [Brown Brazilians](/wiki/Pardo "Pardo") (left to right: two female [mulattoes](/wiki/Mulatto "Mulatto"), two female *[cafuzos](/wiki/Zambo "Zambo")* and a *[caboclo](/wiki/Caboclo "Caboclo")* girl and man). 3rd row: three [Brazilian Indians](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Brazil "Indigenous peoples in Brazil") of different tribes followed by [Afro-Brazilians](/wiki/Afro-Brazilians "Afro-Brazilians") of distinct ethnic background

The *caboclos* formed the majority of the population in the [Northern](/wiki/Northern_Region,_Brazil "Northern Region, Brazil"), [Northeastern](/wiki/Northeast_Region,_Brazil "Northeast Region, Brazil") and [Central-Western](/wiki/Central-West_Region,_Brazil "Central-West Region, Brazil") regions.[[247]](#cite_note-249) A large mulatto population inhabited the eastern coast of the northeastern region from Bahia to Paraíba[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoreira1981108-250)[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAzevedo197174–75-251) and were also present in northern Maranhão,[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarsa_1987,_Vol_10355-252)[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAzevedo197174-253) southern Minas Gerais,[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAzevedo1971161-254) eastern Rio de Janeiro and in [Espírito Santo](/wiki/Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Espírito Santo").[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoreira1981108-250)[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAzevedo1971161-254) The *cafuzos* were the smallest and most difficult to distinguish from the two other mixed-race subgroups since the descendants of *caboclos* and mulattoes also fell into this category and were found in the northeast *sertão* ([hinterland](/wiki/Hinterland "Hinterland")). These groups may still be found in the same areas today.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamos200384-255)

[White Brazilians](/wiki/White_Brazilians "White Brazilians") descended from the original Portuguese settlers. From the 1870s onwards this ethnic group also included other European immigrants: mainly Italians and Germans. Although whites could be found throughout the country, they were the majority group in the [southern region](/wiki/Southern_Region,_Brazil "Southern Region, Brazil") and in São Paulo province.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002133-243) Whites also comprised a significant proportion (40%) of the population in the northeastern provinces of [Ceará](/wiki/Cear%C3%A1 "Ceará"), Paraíba, and [Rio Grande do Norte](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Norte "Rio Grande do Norte").[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002133-243) Afro-Brazilians inhabited the same areas as mulattoes. The majority of the population of Rio de Janeiro, Minas Gerais, Espírito Santo, Bahia, [Sergipe](/wiki/Sergipe "Sergipe"), Alagoas, and [Pernambuco](/wiki/Pernambuco "Pernambuco") provinces (the last four having the smallest percentages of whites in the whole country—less than 30% in each) were black or brown.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002133-243) The Indians, or the indigenous peoples of Brazil, were found mainly in [Piauí](/wiki/Piau%C3%AD "Piauí"), Maranhão, [Pará](/wiki/Par%C3%A1 "Pará"), and [Amazonas](/wiki/Amazonas_(Brazilian_state) "Amazonas (Brazilian state)").[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002133-243)

**Ethnic groups in Brazil** (1835, 1872 and 1890)[[254]](#cite_note-256)[[255]](#cite_note-:0-257)[[256]](#cite_note-:1-258)

| Years | Whites | Browns | Blacks | Indians | Total |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1835 | 24.4% | 18.2% | 51.4% |  | 100% |
| 1872 | 38.1% | 38.3% | 19.7% | 3.9% | 100% |
| 1890 | 44.0% | 32.4% | 14.6% | 9% | 100% |

Because of the existence of distinct racial and cultural communities, 19th century Brazil developed as a multi-ethnic nation. However, there is no reliable information available for the years prior to 1872. The first official national census was compiled by the government in 1872 showing that out of 9,930,479 inhabitants there were 38.1% whites, 38.3% browns, 19.7% blacks and 3.9% Indians.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamos200382-245) The second official national census in 1890 revealed that in a population of 14,333,915, 44% were whites, 32.4% browns, 14.6% blacks and 9% Indians.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamos200382-245)

### European immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: European immigration")]

Main articles: [Immigration to Brazil](/wiki/Immigration_to_Brazil "Immigration to Brazil") and [European immigration to Brazil](/wiki/European_immigration_to_Brazil "European immigration to Brazil")

[![Photograph showing a crowd of people gathered around and on the steps of a white stuccoed house with a hip roof covered in wood shingles situated on a steep slope of a forested hill](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Germans_and_luxembourgers_in_brazil_1875.jpg/250px-Germans_and_luxembourgers_in_brazil_1875.jpg)](/wiki/File:Germans_and_luxembourgers_in_brazil_1875.jpg)

German and Luxembourger immigrants in [Santa Leopoldina](/wiki/Santa_Leopoldina,_Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Santa Leopoldina, Espírito Santo") colony in Espírito Santo province (southeast region), 1875

Prior to 1808, the Portuguese were the only European people to settle Brazil in significant numbers. Although Italians, British, Germans and Spanish had previously immigrated to Brazil, they had only done so as a small number of individuals or in very small groups. These earliest non-Portuguese settlers did not have a significant impact on the culture of Portugal's Brazilian colony.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994511-259) The situation changed after 1808 when King John VI began to encourage immigration from European countries outside Portugal.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994511-259)[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamos200337-260)

The first to arrive in numbers were the Swiss, of whom some 2,000 settled in Rio de Janeiro province (the southeast) during 1818.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994512-261) They were followed by Germans and Irish, who immigrated to Brazil in the 1820s. German settlers gravitated mostly to the southern provinces, where the environment was more like their homeland.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994513-262) In the 1830s, due to the instability of the Regency, European immigration ground to a halt, only recovering after Pedro II took the reins of government and the country entered a period of peace and prosperity.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994513–514-263) Farmers in the southeast, enriched by lucrative coffee exports, created the "partnership system" (a form of [indentured servitude](/wiki/Indentured_servitude "Indentured servitude")) to attract immigrants. The scheme endured until the end of the 1850s, when the system collapsed and was abandoned. The failure was rooted in the large debts European settlers incurred to subsidize their travel and settlement expenses, leaving them as virtual slaves to their employers.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994515-264) Immigration suffered another decline during the Paraguayan War, which lasted from 1864 to 1870.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994517-265)

Immigrant numbers soared during the 1870s in what came to be called the "great immigration". Up to that point, around 10,000 Europeans arrived in Brazil annually, but after 1872, their numbers increased dramatically.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002351-266) It is estimated by the [Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics](/wiki/Brazilian_Institute_of_Geography_and_Statistics "Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics") that 500,000 Europeans immigrated to Brazil between 1808 and 1883.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994633-267) The figure for European settlers arriving between 1884 and 1893 climbed to 883,668.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994633-267) The number of Europeans immigrating continued to rise in the following decades, with 862,100 between 1894 and 1903; and 1,006,617 between 1904 and 1913.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994633-267)

From 1872 until 1879, the nationalities forming the bulk of the new settlers were composed of Portuguese (31.2%), Italians (25.8%), Germans (8.1%) and Spanish (1.9%).[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002351-266) In the 1880s, Italians would surpass the Portuguese (61.8% to 23.3% respectively), and the Spanish would displace the Germans (6.7% to 4.2% respectively).[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002351-266) Other, smaller groups also arrived, including Russians, Poles and Hungarians.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002353-268) Since nearly all European immigrants settled in the southeastern and southern areas of the Empire, ethnic distribution, already unequal before the mass immigration, became even more divergent between regions.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002351–352-269) For a nation that had a small, widely scattered population (4,000,000 in 1823 and 14,333,915 in 1890), the immigration of more than 1,380,000 Europeans had a tremendous effect upon the country's ethnic composition. In 1872, the year of the first reliable national census, white Brazilians represented just over a third (38.1%) of the total population; in 1890, they had increased to a little under half (44.0%) of all Brazilians.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamos200382-245)

### Slavery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Slavery")]

Main article: [Slavery in Brazil](/wiki/Slavery_in_Brazil "Slavery in Brazil")

[![a man and three women seated around a table with two servants standing in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Family_and_slave_house_servants_by_Klumb_1860.png/250px-Family_and_slave_house_servants_by_Klumb_1860.png)](/wiki/File:Family_and_slave_house_servants_by_Klumb_1860.png)

A Brazilian family and its female house slaves, c. 1860

[![a large group of men, women and children holding agricultural implements and standing in front of a long, low building with hills rising in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Slaves_in_coffee_farm_by_marc_ferrez_1885.jpg/250px-Slaves_in_coffee_farm_by_marc_ferrez_1885.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slaves_in_coffee_farm_by_marc_ferrez_1885.jpg)

Slaves and their free children on a coffee farm in Brazil, c. 1885

In 1823, a year after independence, slaves made up 29% of the population of Brazil, a figure which fell throughout the lifetime of the Empire: from 24% in 1854, to 15.2% in 1872, and finally to less than 5% in 1887—the year before slavery was completely abolished.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200218,_239-270) Slaves were mostly adult males from southwestern Africa.[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002237–238-271) Slaves brought to Brazil differed ethnically, religiously and linguistically, each identifying primarily with his or her own nation of origin, rather than by a shared African ethnicity.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200229-272) Some of the [slaves brought to the Americas](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade") had been captured while fighting intertribal wars in Africa and had then been sold to slave dealers.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoxer2002113–114,_116-273)[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200230-274)

Slaves and their descendants were usually found in regions devoted to producing exports for foreign markets.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoxer2002185–186-275) [Sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane") plantations on the eastern coast of the [northeast region](/wiki/Northeast_Region,_Brazil "Northeast Region, Brazil") during the 16th and 17th centuries are typical of economic activities dependent on slave labor.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoxer2002117-276) In northern [Maranhão](/wiki/Maranh%C3%A3o "Maranhão") province, slave labor was used in cotton and rice production in the 18th century.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoxer2002206-277) In this period, slaves were also exploited in [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais") province where gold was extracted.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoxer2002169-278) Slavery was also common in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)") and [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)") during the 19th century for the cultivation of coffee which became vital to the national economy.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002238–239-279) The prevalence of slavery was not geographically uniform across Brazil. Around 1870 only five provinces (Rio de Janeiro with 30%, [Bahia](/wiki/Bahia "Bahia") with 15%, Minas Gerais with 14%, São Paulo with 7% and [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul") also with 7%) held 73% of the nation's total slave population.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002239-280) These were followed by [Pernambuco](/wiki/Pernambuco "Pernambuco") (with 6%) and [Alagoas](/wiki/Alagoas "Alagoas") (with 4%). Among the remaining 13 provinces none individually had even 3%.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet1985167-281)

Most slaves worked as plantation laborers.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002239-280) Relatively few Brazilians owned slaves and most small and medium-sized farms employed free workers.[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFausto1995238–239-282) Slaves could be found scattered throughout society in other capacities: some were used as house servants, farmers, miners, prostitutes, gardeners and in many other roles.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlivieri199943-283) Many emancipated slaves went on to acquire slaves and there were even cases of slaves who had their own slaves.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988194-284)[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007130-285) While slaves were usually black or mulatto there were reported cases of slaves who appeared to be of European descent—the product of generations of inter-ethnic sexual relations between male slave owners and their female mulatto slaves.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlencastro199787–88-286) Even the harshest slave owners adhered to a long-established practice of selling slaves along with their families, taking care not to separate individuals.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet1985170-287) Slaves were regarded by law as properties. The ones who were freed immediately became citizens with all [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights") guaranteed—the only exception being that, until 1881, freed slaves were barred from voting in elections, although their children and descendants could vote.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002239-280)

### Nobility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Nobility")]

Main article: [Brazilian nobility](/wiki/Brazilian_nobility "Brazilian nobility")

[![Looking down upon an assembly in a large, vaulted cathedral with a figure sitting on a large, canopied throne to the left of an altar](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Acclamation_of_Princess_Isabel_1887.jpg/250px-Acclamation_of_Princess_Isabel_1887.jpg)](/wiki/File:Acclamation_of_Princess_Isabel_1887.jpg)

A state ceremony in the [Old Cathedral of Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Old_Cathedral_of_Rio_de_Janeiro "Old Cathedral of Rio de Janeiro"); the attendees are wearing [court dress](/wiki/Court_dress "Court dress")

The [nobility](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") of Brazil differed markedly from its counterparts in Europe: noble titles were not hereditary, with the exception of members of the Imperial Family,[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002553-288) and those who had received a noble title were not considered to belong to a separate social class, and received no [appanages](/wiki/Appanage "Appanage"), [stipends](/wiki/Stipend "Stipend") or [emoluments](/wiki/Emolument "Emolument").[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002553-288) However, many ranks, traditions, and regulations in Brazil's system of nobility were co-opted directly from the Portuguese aristocracy.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002554-289)[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199911-290) During Pedro I's reign there were no clear requisites for someone to be ennobled. During Pedro II's reign (apart from the Regency period during which the regent could not grant titles or honors[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1968208-291)) the nobility evolved into a [meritocracy](/wiki/Meritocracy "Meritocracy")[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002554-289) with titles granted in recognition of an individual's outstanding service to the Empire or for the [public good](/wiki/Common_good "Common good"). Noble rank did not represent "recognition of illustrious ancestry."[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999139-292)[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1968220-293)

It was the emperor's right, as head of the executive branch, to grant titles and honors.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002554-289) The titles of nobility were, in ascending order: baron, viscount, count, marquis, and duke.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002554-289) Apart from position in the hierarchy there were other distinctions between the ranks: counts, marquises and dukes were considered "[Grandees](/wiki/Grandee "Grandee") of the Empire" while the titles of barons and viscounts could be bestowed "with Greatness" or "without Greatness".[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002554-289) All ranks of the Brazilian nobility were to be addressed as *Excelência* (*[Excellency](/wiki/Excellency "Excellency")*).[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002554-289)

Between 1822 and 1889, 986 people were ennobled.[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1968216-294) Only three became dukes: [Auguste de Beauharnais, 2nd Duke of Leuchtenberg](/wiki/Auguste_de_Beauharnais,_2nd_Duke_of_Leuchtenberg "Auguste de Beauharnais, 2nd Duke of Leuchtenberg") (as Duke of Santa Cruz, brother-in-law to Pedro I), Dona Isabel Maria de Alcântara Brasileira (as Duchess of Goiás, illegitimate daughter of Pedro I) and lastly [Luís Alves de Lima e Silva](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Alves_de_Lima_e_Silva,_Duke_of_Caxias "Luís Alves de Lima e Silva, Duke of Caxias") (as Duke of Caxias, commander-in-chief during the [Paraguayan War](/wiki/Paraguayan_War "Paraguayan War")).[[293]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1968204,_206-295) The other titles granted were as follows: 47 marquises, 51 counts, 146 viscounts "with Greatness", 89 viscounts "without Greatness", 135 barons "with Greatness" and 740 barons "without Greatness" resulting in a total of 1,211 noble titles.[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1968218-296) There were fewer nobles than noble titles because many were elevated more than once during their lifetime, such as the Duke of Caxias who was first made a baron, then a count, then a marquis and finally was elevated to a duke.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1968208-291) Grants of nobility were not limited to male Brazilians: [Thomas Cochrane, 10th Earl of Dundonald](/wiki/Thomas_Cochrane,_10th_Earl_of_Dundonald "Thomas Cochrane, 10th Earl of Dundonald"), a Scot, was made Marquis of Maranhão for his role in the [Brazilian War of Independence](/wiki/Brazilian_War_of_Independence "Brazilian War of Independence"),[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1968219-297) and 29 women received grants of nobility in their own right.[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1968221-298) As well as being unrestricted by [gender](/wiki/Gender "Gender"), no [racial](/wiki/Race_(classification_of_human_beings) "Race (classification of human beings)") distinctions were made in conferring noble status. *Caboclos*,[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199977-299) mulattoes,[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1968217-300) blacks[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998191-301) and even Indians[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998191-301) were ennobled.

The lesser nobility, who were untitled, were made up of members of the Imperial Orders. There were six of these: the [Order of Christ](/wiki/Order_of_Christ_(Brazil) "Order of Christ (Brazil)"), the [Order of Saint Benedict of Aviz](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Benedict_of_Aviz "Order of Saint Benedict of Aviz"), the [Order of Saint James of the Sword](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_James_of_the_Sword "Order of Saint James of the Sword"), the [Order of the Southern Cross](/wiki/Order_of_the_Southern_Cross "Order of the Southern Cross"), the [Order of Pedro I](/wiki/Order_of_Pedro_I "Order of Pedro I") and the [Order of the Rose](/wiki/Order_of_the_Rose "Order of the Rose").[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199911-290) The first three had grades of honor beyond the Grand Master (reserved for the Emperor only): knight, commander and grand cross. The latter three, however, had different ranks: the Order of the Southern Cross with four, the Order of the Rose with six, and the Order of Pedro I with three.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199911-290)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![A photograph showing 3 standing men wearing religious habits](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Brazilian_friars_1875.jpg/250px-Brazilian_friars_1875.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brazilian_friars_1875.jpg)

Brazilian [friars](/wiki/Friar "Friar") c. 1875

Article five of the Constitution declared [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") to be the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion").[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002126-302) However, the clergy had long been understaffed, undisciplined and poorly educated,[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254-303)[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007151-304) all of which led to a general loss of respect for the Catholic Church.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254-303) During Pedro II's reign, the Imperial government embarked upon a program of reform designed to address these deficiencies.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254-303) As Catholicism was the official religion, the emperor exercised a great deal of control over Church affairs[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254-303) and paid clerical [stipends](/wiki/Stipend "Stipend"), appointed parish priests, nominated [bishops](/wiki/Bishops_in_the_Catholic_Church "Bishops in the Catholic Church"), ratified [papal bulls](/wiki/Papal_bull "Papal bull") and supervised [seminaries](/wiki/Seminaries "Seminaries").[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254-303)[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007150-305) In pursuing reform, the government selected bishops whose moral fitness, stance on education and support for reform met with their approval.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254-303)[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007151-304) However, as more capable men began to fill the clerical ranks, resentment of government control over the Church increased.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254-303)[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007151-304) Catholic clerics moved closer to the pope and his doctrines. This resulted in the [Religious Issue](/wiki/Religious_Issue "Religious Issue"), a series of clashes during the 1870s between the clergy and the government, since the former wanted a more direct relationship with Rome and the latter sought to maintain its oversight of Church affairs.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254–256-306)

The Constitution did allow followers of other, non-Catholic, faiths to practice their religious beliefs, albeit only in private. The construction of non-Catholic [places of worship](/wiki/Places_of_worship "Places of worship") was officially forbidden.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002450-307) From the outset these restrictions were ignored by both the citizenry and authorities. In [Belém](/wiki/Bel%C3%A9m "Belém"), Pará's capital, the first synagogue was built in 1824.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002450-307) [Jews migrated to Brazil](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Brazil "History of the Jews in Brazil") soon after its independence and settled mainly in the northeastern provinces of Bahia and Pernambuco and in the northern provinces of Amazonas and Pará.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002450-307) Other Jewish groups came from the [Alsace–Lorraine](/wiki/Alsace%E2%80%93Lorraine "Alsace–Lorraine") region of Germany and from Russia.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002450–451-308) By the 1880s, there were several Jewish communities and synagogues scattered throughout Brazil.[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002451-309)

The Protestants were another group that began settling in Brazil at the beginning of the 19th century. The first Protestants were English, and an [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican_Episcopal_Church_of_Brazil "Anglican Episcopal Church of Brazil") church was opened in Rio de Janeiro in 1820. Others were established afterwards in São Paulo, Pernambuco and Bahia provinces.[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002596-310) They were followed by German and Swiss [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutherans "Lutherans") who settled in the South and Southwest regions and built their own houses of worship.[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002596-310) Following the [U.S. Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") in the 1860s, immigrants from the southern United States seeking to escape [Reconstruction](/wiki/Reconstruction_era "Reconstruction era") settled in São Paulo. Several American churches sponsored missionary activities, including Lutherans, [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists"), [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalists "Congregationalists"), and [Methodists](/wiki/Methodists "Methodists").[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002596–597-311)

Among African slaves, Catholicism was the religion of the majority. Most slaves came originally from the midwestern and southwestern portions of the African coast. For over four centuries this region had been the subject of Christian mission activities.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200231-312) Some Africans and their descendants, however, held onto elements of [polytheistic religious traditions](/wiki/Traditional_African_religions "Traditional African religions") by merging them with Catholicism. This resulted in the creation of [syncretic](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") creeds such as [Candomblé](/wiki/Candombl%C3%A9 "Candomblé").[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002114–115-313) [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Brazil "Islam in Brazil") was also practiced among a small minority of African slaves, although it was harshly repressed and by the end of the 19th century had been completely extinguished.[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200230–31-314) By the beginning of the 19th century, the Indians in most of eastern Brazil had been either assimilated or decimated. Some tribes resisted assimilation and either fled farther west, where they were able to maintain their diverse polytheistic beliefs, or were restricted to *aldeamentos* (reservations), where they eventually converted to Catholicism.[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002170-315)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Visual arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Visual arts")]

See also: [Brazilian academic art](/wiki/Brazilian_academic_art "Brazilian academic art")

[![Painting depicting an interior with a nude female model sitting at a spinet piano while an artist works at his easel on the left side of the canvas](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Descanso_do_modelo1.jpg/250px-Descanso_do_modelo1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Descanso_do_modelo1.jpg)

*O descanso do modelo* (The model's rest), by [Almeida Júnior](/wiki/Almeida_J%C3%BAnior "Almeida Júnior"), 1882

[![A landscape painting depicting houses nestled among trees in the middle-distance, and a large hill topped by a rock spire in the far distance](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Joaquim_Jos%C3%A9_da_Fran%C3%A7a_J%C3%BAnior%2C_Morro_da_Vi%C3%BAva%2C_circa_1888.jpg/250px-Joaquim_Jos%C3%A9_da_Fran%C3%A7a_J%C3%BAnior%2C_Morro_da_Vi%C3%BAva%2C_circa_1888.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joaquim_Jos%C3%A9_da_Fran%C3%A7a_J%C3%BAnior,_Morro_da_Vi%C3%BAva,_circa_1888.jpg)

*Morro da Viúva* (Widow's mount), by [França Júnior](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7a_J%C3%BAnior "França Júnior"), c. 1888

According to historian Ronald Raminelli, "visual arts underwent huge innovations in the Empire in comparison to the colonial period."[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200283-316) With independence in 1822, painting, sculpture and architecture were influenced by national symbols and the monarchy, as both surpassed religious themes in their importance. The previously dominant [old Baroque style](/wiki/Baroque_architecture_in_Portugal "Baroque architecture in Portugal") was superseded by [Neoclassicism](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism").[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200283-316) New developments appeared, such as the use of iron in architecture and the appearance of [lithography](/wiki/Lithography "Lithography") and photography, which revitalized the visual arts.[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200283-316)

The government's creation of the [Imperial Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/Imperial_Academy_of_Fine_Arts_(Brazil) "Imperial Academy of Fine Arts (Brazil)") in the 1820s played a pivotal role in influencing and expanding the visual arts in Brazil, mainly by educating generations of artists but also by serving as a stylistic guideline.[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200284-317) The academy's origins lay in the foundation of the *Escola Real das Ciências, Artes e Ofícios* (Royal School of the Sciences, Arts and Crafts) in 1816 by the Portuguese King John VI. Its members—of whom the most famous was [Jean-Baptiste Debret](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Debret "Jean-Baptiste Debret")—were French [émigrées](/wiki/%C3%89migr%C3%A9 "Émigré") who worked as painters, sculptors, musicians and engineers.[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200221–22-318) The school's main goal was to encourage French [aesthetics](/wiki/Aesthetics "Aesthetics") and the Neoclassical style to replace the prevalent baroque style.[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200222-319) Plagued by a lack of funds since its inception, the school was later renamed as the Academy of Fine Arts in 1820, and in 1824 received its final name under the Empire: Imperial Academy of Fine Arts.[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200222-319)

It was only following Pedro II's majority in 1840, however, that the academy became a powerhouse, part of the Emperor's greater scheme of fomenting a national culture and consequently uniting all Brazilians in a common sense of nationhood.[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998126–127-320) Pedro II would sponsor the Brazilian culture through several public institutions funded by the government (not restricted to the Academy of Fine Arts), such as [Brazilian Historic and Geographic Institute](/wiki/Brazilian_Historic_and_Geographic_Institute "Brazilian Historic and Geographic Institute")[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998126-321) and Imperial Academy of Music and National Opera.[[320]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998152-322) That sponsorship would pave the way not only for the careers of artists, but also for those engaged in other fields, including historians such as [Francisco Adolfo de Varnhagen](/wiki/Francisco_Adolfo_de_Varnhagen "Francisco Adolfo de Varnhagen")[[321]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002285-323) and musicians such as the operatic composer [Antônio Carlos Gomes](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Carlos_Gomes "Antônio Carlos Gomes").[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002123-324)

By the 1840s, [Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") had largely supplanted Neoclassicism, not only in painting, but also in sculpture and architecture.[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200284-317) The academy did not resume its role of simply providing education: prizes, medals, scholarships in foreign countries and funding were used as incentives.[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998145-325) Among its staff and students were some of the most renowned Brazilian artists, including [Simplício Rodrigues de Sá](/wiki/Simpl%C3%ADcio_Rodrigues_de_S%C3%A1 "Simplício Rodrigues de Sá"), [Félix Taunay](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Taunay "Félix Taunay"), [Manuel de Araújo Porto-alegre](/wiki/Manuel_de_Ara%C3%BAjo_Porto-alegre,_Baron_of_Santo_%C3%82ngelo "Manuel de Araújo Porto-alegre, Baron of Santo Ângelo"), [Pedro Américo](/wiki/Pedro_Am%C3%A9rico "Pedro Américo"), [Victor Meirelles](/wiki/Victor_Meirelles "Victor Meirelles"), [Rodolfo Amoedo](/wiki/Rodolfo_Amoedo "Rodolfo Amoedo"), [Almeida Júnior](/wiki/Almeida_J%C3%BAnior "Almeida Júnior"), [Rodolfo Bernardelli](/wiki/Rodolfo_Bernardelli "Rodolfo Bernardelli"), and [João Zeferino da Costa](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Zeferino_da_Costa "João Zeferino da Costa").[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998145-325)[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200284–85-326) In the 1880s, after having been long regarded as the official style of the academy, Romanticism declined, and other styles were explored by a new generation of artists. Among the new genres was [Landscape art](/wiki/Landscape_art "Landscape art"), the most famous exponents of which were [Georg Grimm](/wiki/Georg_Grimm "Georg Grimm"), [Giovanni Battista Castagneto](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Castagneto "Giovanni Battista Castagneto"), [França Júnior](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7a_J%C3%BAnior "França Júnior"), and [Antônio Parreiras](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Parreiras "Antônio Parreiras").[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200285-327) Another style which gained popularity in the fields of painting and architecture was [Eclecticism](/wiki/Eclecticism "Eclecticism").[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas200285-327)

### Literature and theater

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Empire_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Literature and theater")]

[![An old photograph depicting two dark-haired men seated in the foreground and a white-haired man standing behind](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Three_brazilian_writers_1858.jpg/250px-Three_brazilian_writers_1858.jpg)](/wiki/File:Three_brazilian_writers_1858.jpg)

A photograph dating from c. 1858, showing three major Brazilian [Romantic](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") writers. From left to right: [Gonçalves Dias](/wiki/Gon%C3%A7alves_Dias "Gonçalves Dias"), [Manuel de Araújo Porto Alegre](/wiki/Manuel_de_Ara%C3%BAjo_Porto_Alegre,_Baron_of_Santo_%C3%82ngelo "Manuel de Araújo Porto Alegre, Baron of Santo Ângelo") and [Gonçalves de Magalhães](/wiki/Gon%C3%A7alves_de_Magalh%C3%A3es,_Viscount_of_Araguaia "Gonçalves de Magalhães, Viscount of Araguaia")

In the first years after independence, Brazilian literature was still heavily influenced by Portuguese literature and its predominant Neoclassical style.[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002482-328) In 1837, [Gonçalves de Magalhães](/wiki/Gon%C3%A7alves_de_Magalh%C3%A3es,_Viscount_of_Araguaia "Gonçalves de Magalhães, Viscount of Araguaia") published the first work of Romanticism in Brazil, beginning a new era in the nation.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002661-329) The next year, 1838, saw the first play performed by Brazilians with a national theme, which marked the birth of Brazilian theater. Until then themes were often based on European works even if not performed by foreign actors.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002661-329) Romanticism at that time was regarded as the literary style that best fitted Brazilian literature, which could reveal its uniqueness when compared to foreign literature.[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002482–483-330) During the 1830s and 1840s, "a network of newspapers, journals, book publishers and printing houses emerged which together with the opening of theaters in the major towns brought into being what could be termed, but for the narrowness of its scope, a national culture".[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988237-331)

Romanticism reached its apogee between the late 1850s and the early 1870s as it divided into several branches, including [Indianism](/wiki/Indianism_(arts) "Indianism (arts)") and [sentimentalism](/wiki/Sentimentalism_(literature) "Sentimentalism (literature)").[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002483-332) The most influential literary style in 19th-century Brazil, many of the most renowned Brazilian writers were exponents of Romanticism: [Manuel de Araújo Porto Alegre](/wiki/Manuel_de_Ara%C3%BAjo_Porto_Alegre,_Baron_of_Santo_%C3%82ngelo "Manuel de Araújo Porto Alegre, Baron of Santo Ângelo"),[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002513-333) [Gonçalves Dias](/wiki/Gon%C3%A7alves_Dias "Gonçalves Dias"), Gonçalves de Magalhães, [José de Alencar](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_de_Alencar "José de Alencar"), [Bernardo Guimarães](/wiki/Bernardo_Guimar%C3%A3es "Bernardo Guimarães"), [Álvares de Azevedo](/wiki/%C3%81lvares_de_Azevedo "Álvares de Azevedo"), [Casimiro de Abreu](/wiki/Casimiro_de_Abreu "Casimiro de Abreu"), [Castro Alves](/wiki/Castro_Alves "Castro Alves"), [Joaquim Manuel de Macedo](/wiki/Joaquim_Manuel_de_Macedo "Joaquim Manuel de Macedo"), [Manuel Antônio de Almeida](/wiki/Manuel_Ant%C3%B4nio_de_Almeida "Manuel Antônio de Almeida"), and [Alfredo d'Escragnolle Taunay](/wiki/Alfredo_d%27Escragnolle_Taunay,_Viscount_of_Taunay "Alfredo d'Escragnolle Taunay, Viscount of Taunay").[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002484-334) In theater, the most famous Romanticist playwrights were [Martins Pena](/wiki/Martins_Pena "Martins Pena")[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002484-334) and [Joaquim Manuel de Macedo](/wiki/Joaquim_Manuel_de_Macedo "Joaquim Manuel de Macedo").[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002691-335) Brazilian Romanticism did not have the same success in theater as it had in literature, as most of the plays were either Neoclassic tragedies or Romantic works from Portugal or translations from Italian, French or Spanish.[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002691-335) After the opening of the Brazilian Dramatic Conservatory in 1845, the government gave financial aid to national theater companies in exchange for staging plays in Portuguese.[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002691-335)

By the 1880s, Romanticism was superseded by new literary styles. The first to appear was [Realism](/wiki/Literary_realism "Literary realism"), which had among its most notable writers [Machado de Assis](/wiki/Machado_de_Assis "Machado de Assis") and [Raul Pompeia](/wiki/Raul_Pompeia "Raul Pompeia").[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002483-332) Newer styles that coexisted with Realism, [Naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(literature) "Naturalism (literature)") and [Parnassianism](/wiki/Parnassianism "Parnassianism"), were both connected to the former's evolution.[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002483-332) Among the best-known Naturalists were [Aluísio Azevedo](/wiki/Alu%C3%ADsio_Azevedo "Aluísio Azevedo") and [Adolfo Caminha](/wiki/Adolfo_Caminha "Adolfo Caminha").[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002483–484-336) Notable Parnassians were [Gonçalves Crespo](/wiki/Gon%C3%A7alves_Crespo "Gonçalves Crespo"), [Alberto de Oliveira](/wiki/Alberto_de_Oliveira "Alberto de Oliveira"), [Raimundo Correia](/wiki/Raimundo_Correia "Raimundo Correia"), and [Olavo Bilac](/wiki/Olavo_Bilac "Olavo Bilac").[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002484-334) Brazilian theater became influenced by Realism in 1855, decades earlier than the style's impact upon literature and poetry.[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002692-337) Famous Realist playwrights included José de Alencar, [Quintino Bocaiuva](/wiki/Quintino_Bocaiuva "Quintino Bocaiuva"), Joaquim Manuel de Macedo, [Júlia Lopes de Almeida](/wiki/J%C3%BAlia_Lopes_de_Almeida "Júlia Lopes de Almeida"), and [Maria Angélica Ribeiro](/wiki/Maria_Ang%C3%A9lica_Ribeiro "Maria Angélica Ribeiro").[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002692-337) Brazilian plays staged by national companies competed for audiences alongside foreign plays and companies.[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002693-338) Performing arts in Imperial Brazil also encompassed the staging of musical duets, dancing, gymnastics, comedy and farces.[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002693-338) Less prestigious, but more popular with the working classes were puppeteers and magicians, as well as the circus, with its travelling companies of performers, including acrobats, trained animals, illusionists, and other stunt-oriented artists.[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002694-339)

1. **[^](#cite_ref-175)** The matter of the contested regions were later settled peacefully in favor of Brazil's pretentions. The exception was the border with Argentina next to the Brazilian province of Santa Catarina, in which half of its territory were divided amicably between Brazil and its neighbor. The current-day state of [Acre](/wiki/Acre_(state) "Acre (state)"), formerly part of Bolivia, was purchased by Brazil in 1903 and had not been claimed during the imperial era.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-187)** The only exceptions regarding border disputes in the north and west were minor diplomatic disputes with France and Britain in the northern region. During the 1830s, both countries occupied and unsuccessfully attempted to claim some areas in the north as part of their colonial empires. See [Viana 1994](#CITEREFViana1994), p. 575.
